sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presented to the office with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his 10-year-old son.
He reported landing awkwardly after being jumped on and twisting his ankle, resulting in bruising and swelling on the outside of the ankle.
He described difficulty walking and instability in the ankle, which he attributed to the recent resumption of his workout routine.
He is concerned about disrupting his progress and plans to continue coaching his son's basketball team starting in April.
Past medical history: NOT MENTIONED
Review of systems: NOT MENTIONED
Current medications: NOT MENTIONED
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's ankle was swollen on the lateral side, with exquisite tenderness to palpation laterally and some tenderness over the medial deltoid region.
The skin was intact, and capillary refill was brisk.
The ankle did not exhibit malalignment.
Test Results: The patient had an X-ray taken, which revealed a displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a displaced lateral malleolar fracture of the right ankle, which is unstable and requires surgical intervention.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; unstable ankle; probable need for surgery.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam for pain and inflammation, and instructed the patient to ice and elevate the ankle.
I recommended that the patient use crutches to avoid putting weight on the ankle.
I advised the patient to continue coaching his son's basketball team, as the surgery will not prevent him from doing so.
I estimated that the patient will be out for approximately three months, and will need to follow up with further treatment and rehabilitation after surgery.
Follow-up: I instructed the patient to return to the office after surgery for follow-up care and rehabilitation.
